cross_connections.display_label: Susannah Judd's family name connects to the Judd family of.

bio.notes: Widow of Samuel Seymour of Kensington before marrying Elijah Hooker

career.location: Morristown, St. Lawrence County, New York
career.notes: Bought Arnold Smith hotel fall 1815. Sold to Alfred Hooker Aug 23, 1823.
First collector of port. Overseer of the poor when town organized March 27, 1821.

narrative_blocks.header: St. Lawrence River hotel owner; first port collector
narrative_blocks.body: John Hooker moved his family from Turin to Morristown in the fall of 1815 — a village of only a few houses on the St. Lawrence River — where he bought Arnold Smith's hotel and ran it until 1823. When the town was organized on March 27, 1821, he was appointed overseer of the poor and became its first collector of the port.

narrative_blocks.header: From Connecticut to Turin to the St. Lawrence
narrative_blocks.body: John Hooker left Kensington, Connecticut after his first wife Abigail Goodrich died in 1794, remarried Mary Ives in 1795, and removed the family to Turin, New York in 1802 — then in 1814 to Morristown, beautifully situated on the St. Lawrence River, where he died April 12, 1829.

narrative_blocks.header: Child Olive outlived him by seven years
narrative_blocks.body: John Hooker Jr. married Lucy Coffin on November 1, 1815 and died at Morristown December 14, 1825 at thirty-four. His only child Olive survived him by seven years, dying at Morristown July 23, 1832.

narrative_blocks.header: Both died in South America; son returned alone
narrative_blocks.body: Abigail Hooker married Chester Hoadley in 1812, and in 1838 they traveled to South America on business — taking their eldest child — when both she and Chester died a few months after arriving. Their son returned to the United States alone; the Hoadley genealogy records that the children were born in Alexander, New York.

career.notes: Appointed Tyler; reappointed Lincoln and Grant.

narrative_blocks.header: Customs collector appointed by Tyler, Lincoln, and Grant
narrative_blocks.body: Henry Hooker served as Collector of Customs at the Port of Morristown, appointed by President Tyler and reappointed by Presidents Lincoln and Grant — spanning decades across the Civil War era. He was also Justice of the Peace, town Supervisor, Episcopal Church Warden, and partner in the mercantile firm Miller, Hooker & Co.

narrative_blocks.header: Son Alfred killed at Monocacy, 1864
narrative_blocks.body: Henry Hooker's son Alfred Ives became Captain of Company B, 106th New York Infantry, and was killed at the Battle of Monocacy on July 9, 1864 — the engagement that slowed Jubal Early's Confederate march on Washington. Alfred died three years before his mother Melinda and eighteen years before his father.

narrative_blocks.header: Left Yale; built Canada's pioneer forwarding business
narrative_blocks.body: Alfred Hooker was educated at Yale but left without graduating to enter commercial life, establishing Hooker & Henderson at Prescott, Ontario — directly across the St. Lawrence from Ogdensburg, New York. The firm grew into Hooker & Hilton and then Hooker & Jaques, known province-wide, until the Grand Trunk Railroad destroyed it.

narrative_blocks.header: Naturalized British; Warden of Prescott's English Church
narrative_blocks.body: Alfred Hooker was naturalized as a British citizen in 1821 and became deeply rooted in Prescott, advancing its schools and religious institutions and serving as Warden of the English Church. He died August 27, 1880; his wife Elvira died two days later in Toronto.

narrative_blocks.header: Moved to Brockville; ten children, most lost
narrative_blocks.body: Mary Ann Hooker married John Clark Potter in 1822 and moved to Brockville, Ontario, where Potter dealt in shoes and leather and became a naturalized British citizen. Of their ten children born at Brockville between 1823 and 1845, six died in infancy or early youth.

narrative_blocks.header: Died at six years old in Morristown, 1816
narrative_blocks.body: Lucretia Hooker was born December 10, 1810 in Turin, New York and died December 26, 1816 at six years old in Morristown on the St. Lawrence. The family had moved there from Turin in 1814, two years before her death.

narrative_blocks.header: Died in Kingston, Canada at fifty-one
narrative_blocks.body: Elijah Hooker was born February 13, 1814 in Turin, New York and died January 29, 1866 in Kingston, Ontario at fifty-one. He was the youngest of John Hooker's seven children by Mary Ives, born the year the family moved to Morristown.

cross_connections.display_label: Co-widow in the General Hart house thirty-eight years
cross_connections.notes: After Cyprian Hart died March 1, 1806 and General Selah Hart died June 10, 1806 in the same house, Lucy (then 34) and Ruth Cole Hart (then 63) — wives of nephew and adoptive uncle — kept the General Hart homestead together for as long as they lived. Ruth died there January 15, 1844 at 101 years, 2 months, 16 days. Lucy lived another seven years alone, dying July 8, 1851 at 79. EH #537: 'his widow and the widow of his uncle Gen. Selah Hart occupied the General Hart house and property together as long as they lived.'

burial.plot_notes: Ledge Cemetery, Berlin CT. FindAGrave Memorial #61935595.

narrative_blocks.header: Widowed at thirty-four with six young children
narrative_blocks.body: She lost her husband Cyprian Hart on March 1, 1806 and his adoptive uncle General Selah Hart fourteen weeks later on June 10. By the adoption arrangement she remained mistress of the General Hart homestead with her six children, the youngest only nine months old. She had all six baptized at Kensington church the following November.

narrative_blocks.header: Forty-five years a widow, three children unmarried
narrative_blocks.body: Of her six children, three died unmarried before their forty-second year: Selah at forty-one in 1838, the physician Henry Cyprian at thirty in 1831, and Emily at twenty-nine in 1835. She herself died July 8, 1851 at Kensington aged seventy-nine — having outlived her husband by forty-five years and having shared the General Hart house with her co-widow Ruth Cole Hart for thirty-eight of them.

burial.plot_notes: Ledge Cemetery, Berlin CT. FindAGrave Memorial #61935571.

cross_connections.display_label: Connecticut Militia General who took him in at four
cross_connections.notes: Cyprian's father Nathaniel Hart No. 2 died October 13, 1773 when Cyprian was four. His childless uncle Gen. Selah Hart (whose only natural child Mary had died young) adopted him as heir to the General Hart homestead. Cyprian died March 1, 1806; Selah died fourteen weeks later on June 10, 1806.

cross_connections.display_label: Two Cyprian Harts, both adopted by Gen. Selah Hart
cross_connections.notes: H00562 (this entry) and H00568 both bore the name Cyprian Hart and both were documented as adopted heirs of General Selah Hart (H00273) — a striking naming + adoption coincidence.

narrative_blocks.header: Father dead at four, raised on uncle's homestead
narrative_blocks.body: When Nathaniel Hart died at Kensington on October 13, 1773, his sixth son Cyprian was four years old. Widowed Martha Norton soon removed to Hartford, and the boy was taken in by his childless uncle General Selah Hart and raised on the General Hart homestead as adopted heir.

narrative_blocks.header: Married Lucy Hooker at twenty-three, both born 1772
narrative_blocks.body: On December 24, 1795 he married Lucy Hooker of the same Kensington parish, born four months and ten days after him in January 1772. They were second cousins through their shared great-grandfather, the Honorable John Hooker of Farmington. Six children arrived in nine years.

narrative_blocks.header: Died before the General who had raised him
narrative_blocks.body: He died at Kensington on March 1, 1806, aged thirty-seven, leaving his youngest daughter Emily nine months old. General Selah Hart followed him into the grave fourteen weeks later, on June 10, in the same house. By the adoption arrangement the General Hart estate passed through his nephew's six children rather than to Selah's collateral kin.

narrative_blocks.header: Two widows shared the house thirty-eight years
narrative_blocks.body: After both husbands died in the spring of 1806, his widow Lucy Hooker Hart and his adoptive aunt Ruth Cole Hart kept the Kensington homestead together for the rest of their lives. Ruth died in the house in January 1844 at one hundred and one. Lucy lived another seven years alone, dying there in July 1851.

naming_inspiration.description: Eldest son named for his father's adoptive uncle General Selah Hart, in whose Kensington homestead he was raised from birth.

narrative_blocks.header: Named for the General who adopted his father
narrative_blocks.body: Eldest son of Cyprian Hart and Lucy Hooker, born October 19, 1796 in the General Hart homestead at Kensington. His given name honored his father's adoptive uncle General Selah Hart, then sixty-four years old and living in the same house. He carried the General's name through forty-one unmarried years and died at Kensington on May 24, 1838.

burial.plot_notes: South Burying Ground, Kensington CT. FindAGrave Memorial #15158620.

narrative_blocks.header: Married a Wallingford captain as his third wife
narrative_blocks.body: On March 25, 1829 at Kensington she married Captain Albert Norton of Wallingford, son of Roger and Hannah Rice Norton. She was his third wife — his first Lucy Lee died in 1824, his second Rebecca Eddy in 1828, both buried at Kensington. Ruth was thirty-two; Albert was thirty-nine.

narrative_blocks.header: Buried first daughter, named the next after her
narrative_blocks.body: Her first daughter Isabella was born October 15, 1831 and died at Kensington on December 15, 1840, aged nine. Two and a half years later, on July 5, 1843, she bore a second daughter and named her Harriet Isabel — the middle name carrying the dead sister's name forward.

cross_connections.display_label: Inherited his homestead two generations after his death
cross_connections.notes: The General Hart homestead at Kensington descended through three generations: built and lived in by Gen. Selah Hart (d. 1806); held by his adopted nephew Cyprian Hart (Abigail's father) and his cohabiting widows Lucy Hooker and Ruth Cole Hart for thirty-eight years; passed to Abigail and her husband Oliver Moore upon her mother's death in 1851. Hart Genealogy explicitly identifies the property: 'where the late Oliver Moore lived.'

narrative_blocks.header: Inherited the General Hart homestead through marriage
narrative_blocks.body: On March 25, 1829 at Kensington she married Oliver Moore. The General Hart house at Kensington — built by her great-uncle General Selah Hart, held by her adopted-heir father Cyprian Hart from 1773 to 1806, then by her widowed mother and great-aunt Ruth Cole Hart together for thirty-eight years — became the Moore residence by the time of her own death in 1850. Three generations of Hart property descent ended at her household.

naming_inspiration.description: Middle name honors his father Cyprian Hart, who died when Henry was five years old.

institutions.institution_blurb: Yale Medical M.D. 1826; began practice in his native Kensington parish.

burial.plot_notes: Ledge Cemetery, Berlin CT. FindAGrave Memorial #62169513.

education.notes: M.D. 1826. Attended two courses of lectures per Yale Biographical Notices.

career.organization: Private practice, Kensington Parish, Berlin CT
career.notes: Started practice in his native parish immediately after receiving M.D. Died in practice March 28, 1831 age 30.

narrative_blocks.header: Raised by his widowed mother and adoptive great-aunt
narrative_blocks.body: Born January 30, 1801 at the General Hart homestead in Kensington, fifth child and younger son of Cyprian Hart and Lucy Hooker. His father died March 1, 1806 when Henry was five years and one month old. He was raised in the household of his widowed mother Lucy and her adoptive aunt Ruth Cole Hart, who together kept the homestead from 1806 onward.

narrative_blocks.header: Yale Medical degree at twenty-five, physician at twenty-six
narrative_blocks.body: He graduated from Yale College in the Class of 1822 at age twenty-one, then attended two courses of lectures at the Yale Medical School and received the M.D. degree in 1826 in his twenty-fifth year. He began practice in his native Kensington parish almost immediately.

narrative_blocks.header: A man of high promise, dead at thirty
narrative_blocks.body: He practiced medicine in Kensington for five years and died there on March 28, 1831, aged thirty, unmarried. The Yale Biographical Notices summary records him as regarded as a man of high promise. His sister Emily, also unmarried, would die at the same Kensington parish four years later at age twenty-nine.

burial.plot_notes: Walnut Grove Cemetery, Meriden CT. FindAGrave Memorial #13256921.

naming_inspiration.description: Given the full name of her mother Lucy Hooker Hart.

narrative_blocks.header: Named for her mother; married into Blue Hills
narrative_blocks.body: Born November 14, 1803 at Kensington, fifth child and youngest daughter, given the full name of her mother Lucy Hooker Hart. On November 1, 1826 — two weeks before her twenty-third birthday — she married Sheldon Peck of Blue Hills, Kensington, son of Lemuel and Lydia Dickinson Peck, nine years her senior. Five children followed.

narrative_blocks.header: Died at Meriden four months after her husband
narrative_blocks.body: Late in life Sheldon grew infirm and the couple removed from Kensington to Meriden, where he died July 31, 1872 aged seventy-eight. She survived him by three months and twelve days, dying at Meriden on November 12, 1872 aged sixty-nine. Both were buried at Walnut Grove Cemetery in Meriden, alongside her husband and the children who survived them.

narrative_blocks.header: Father died ten months after she was born
narrative_blocks.body: Born May 19, 1805 at Kensington, youngest of six children. Her father Cyprian Hart was already in failing health and died March 1, 1806 when she was nine months and eleven days old. She knew her father only as the children's memory of a deathbed.

narrative_blocks.header: Died unmarried at twenty-nine, like brother Henry Cyprian
narrative_blocks.body: She died at Kensington on April 3, 1835 aged twenty-nine, unmarried — four years and six days after her physician brother Henry Cyprian Hart had died in the same parish at age thirty, also unmarried. Of Cyprian and Lucy's six children, three died unmarried before forty-two: Selah at forty-one, Henry Cyprian at thirty, Emily at twenty-nine.

landmarks.landmark_blurb: Grew up in his father Elijah's Kensington house.

burial.plot_notes: Evergreen Cemetery, New Haven — Section Walnut Avenue, Plot 1243 South.

institutions.institution_blurb: Founded the New Haven carriage firm that became Henry Hooker & Co.

landmarks.landmark_blurb: Owned and modernized the Kensington homestead; the house bears his name.

narrative_blocks.header: Sold Northern carriages South, then built his own
narrative_blocks.body: He began in 1830 by opening a repository in a Southern city to sell Northern-made carriages, then returned to New Haven to manufacture them himself under a series of partnerships that became Henry Hooker & Company, incorporated in 1868. By the Gilded Age the plant was among the largest carriage works in the country, with a steady export trade abroad.

narrative_blocks.header: Kept the Kensington homestead as a summer house
narrative_blocks.body: As head of Henry Hooker & Company, a large and widely known New Haven carriage-building firm, he moved his family to the city but kept the old family home at Kensington as a summer residence. The firm passed to both his sons, Frank Henry and Norman Albert.

landmarks.landmark_blurb: Helped raise the monument on which his fallen son is named.

narrative_blocks.header: Never recovered from his soldier son's death
narrative_blocks.body: His son Frank, a Union captain, died of a battle wound in 1864; by family account Ira never recovered from the loss and died a few years afterward. He had helped raise the Kensington Soldiers' Monument, on which Frank's name is engraved.

narrative_blocks.header: Widowed six months after his first wedding
narrative_blocks.body: Sarah Griswold, whom he married in 1808, died about six months later. He then followed his brother John to upstate New York, settling at Carthage, where he kept a large country store and served more than twenty years as postmaster.

narrative_blocks.header: New York merchant who died in New Orleans
narrative_blocks.body: Raised at Carthage, he lived about a decade each at Champion and Montague in upstate New York before moving south. He died in New Orleans on the first day of 1900.

narrative_blocks.header: Calista died young
narrative_blocks.body: Calista died in 1832 at the age of one.

narrative_blocks.header: Lost both her young sons within three days
narrative_blocks.body: Her two boys, Edward and William, died within three days of each other in April 1860, aged six and four. She herself died two years later, and her widower went on to marry her younger sister.

narrative_blocks.header: Married her late sister's widower
narrative_blocks.body: After her elder sister Mary Sophia died in 1862, she married Mary's widower, the New York lawyer William Bartlett Smith, in 1863.

cross_connections.display_label: co-wrote his schoolbooks and founded America's deaf school

bio.title: Reverend
bio.bio_blurb: Hartford minister, editor, and children's author

institutions.institution_blurb: Chaplain at end of life; died there December 17, 1864.

narrative_blocks.header: He continued Gallaudet's Bible series after Gallaudet died
narrative_blocks.body: In collaboration with Rev. Thomas Hopkins Gallaudet — the pioneer of American deaf education who co-founded the American School for the Deaf in Hartford — Horace Hooker co-authored The Practical Spelling Book and The School and Family Dictionary, two educational works that circulated widely in New England schools and were reprinted under both names for decades after Gallaudet's death in 1851. When Gallaudet died, Hooker continued the Scripture Biographies for the Young series his collaborator had begun — completing it as an act of literary stewardship that kept both names in circulation in Connecticut's religious education networks through the Civil War era, and that reflected his role as one of Hartford's most productive clerical authors across four decades.

narrative_blocks.header: Secretary of CT Missionary Society for twenty years
narrative_blocks.body: For more than twenty years he served as secretary of the Connecticut Missionary Society. He ended his life as chaplain of the Hartford insane retreat — where he died December 17, 1864, having ministered there until the end.

narrative_blocks.header: Founding student of Yale's theological department
narrative_blocks.body: As a Yale tutor in 1817 he joined the small group of students who studied theology informally under Professor Eleazar T. Fitch — the cohort that became the founding class of what grew into Yale Divinity School. The twelve students formalized the program by inviting Professor Kingsley to teach Hebrew and Professor Goodrich elocution and sermon composition, transforming twice-weekly evenings at Fitch's house into Yale's first theological department.

institutions.institution_blurb: Yale College, B.A. 1864; Phi Beta Kappa.

cross_connections.display_label: shared the Yale Class of 1864 and ran the carriage firm
was a Yale 1864 classmate who married his sister
His niece's son Abel Hollister came under his guardianship

narrative_blocks.header: Named for a brother who died nine days
narrative_blocks.body: His older brother John was born and died in December 1693 — living only nine days. When the next son arrived in 1695, his parents gave him the same name. He carried it through 71 years, Justice of the Peace and prominent Kensington businessman.

narrative_blocks.header: He and his three sisters all married into
narrative_blocks.body: His sisters Abigail, Mary, and Sarah married three sons of Capt. John Hart between 1719 and 1725. John himself had already married Mercy Hart in 1728 — from a different Hart branch, through Dea. Ebenezer Hart. Four Hooker siblings, two separate Hart lines, one generation.

narrative_blocks.header: Justice of the Peace
narrative_blocks.body: EH describes him as "a prominent and active man in the business of the town." He held the office of Justice of the Peace in Kensington, a parish of Berlin CT, and died there on August 3, 1766, age 71.

parents.father_research_notes: EH says father was Deacon Thomas Hart — WRONG. Barbour Collection Farmington VR confirms father was Dea. Ebenezer Hart (X00119). Primary source wins.

sources.notes: Barbour Collection confirms: father = Dea. Ebenezer Hart

bio.notes: EH gives 'Mercy (Mary) Hart' — both names used interchangeably

sources.full: L.J. Hooker, 'Edward Hooker, Commander, U.S. Navy,' The Old Northwest Genealogical Quarterly (Columbus, Ohio, 1898-), VII, 12

cross_connections.notes: Col. Wadsworth's son Daniel Wadsworth founded the Wadsworth Atheneum in Hartford — a monument with Hooker family kinship interest. Research needed to confirm Mehitable Russell's Hooker descent line. Warren 1887 confirms: Elizabeth Standley married John Wadsworth — their son Daniel Wadsworth founded the Wadsworth Atheneum.
cross_connections.display_label: was his father-in-law, honored in the Indian wars

cross_connections.notes: Capt. Thomas Hart b. ca. 1644, d. Aug 27, 1726 age 82. Son of Stephen Hart (X00034). Brother of John Hart who survived the 1666 fire (X00033). Not in this tree through marriage — noted as civic context only.
cross_connections.display_label: helped govern Farmington alongside him for decades
married a descendant of both Hooker and Standley
served Hartford’s founding-family public life alongside him
was his wife’s sister and Col. Jeremiah Wadsworth’s grandmother
fathered four children who married four of his own
Both served as Connecticut Colony Supreme Court judges.
was the cousin whose Indian Neck deed he registered
signed a Tunxis dispossession deed as his descendant’s in-law
shared his entanglement in Tunxis affairs, decades apart
was called with him one of Farmington’s two prominent men

burial.plot_notes: Memento Mori Cemetery, Farmington, Hartford County, Connecticut. Same cemetery as his father Rev. Samuel Hooker (H00007).

notable.primary_url_label: Native Northeast Portal — John Hooker biography

narrative_blocks.header: Judge of Connecticut Colony's Supreme Court
narrative_blocks.body: He served as Judge of the Supreme Court of Connecticut Colony from 1724 to 1732 and as Speaker of the Lower House. He also served as witness, certifying Assistant, and Registrar in the 1733 Sue Squa land deed — three official roles in a single transaction.

narrative_blocks.header: He defended Tunxis land rights in court
narrative_blocks.body: In 1738, John Hooker provided a formal statement to the Connecticut General Court that ninety acres at Indian Neck had 'always belonged' to the Tunxis tribe — an unusual act of testimony on behalf of the indigenous people whose land his own ancestors had settled near.

narrative_blocks.header: The elm fell so someone could walk straight
narrative_blocks.body: His father gave him a site on the west side of Farmington's main street, where he built under the branches of a great elm. The tree became famous through all the region for the company that gathered beneath it — three generations of Hookers lived in its shade before a later owner cut it down to straighten the sidewalk.

narrative_blocks.header: The bell tolled — Hooker stepped out first
narrative_blocks.body: A chronicler recorded that every Sunday the bell rang, then began tolling; at the first stroke, Hooker issued from his house and proceeded to the Meeting House. The minister walked from his house. Whoever arrived first waited at the door; together they entered — at which the bell stopped and all the congregation arose.

narrative_blocks.header: The Sabbath ended when Hooker touched his gate
narrative_blocks.body: The holiness of Sunday was supposed to end at sunset — but no child in the town dared stir until John Hooker gave the signal. When the sun sank, he walked to his gate and leaned on it; open flew the doors and out came the children with a wild rush and shout.

narrative_blocks.header: Dark hair, dark eyes — dignity commanding respect
narrative_blocks.body: His grandson described him: "Dark hair and dark eyes, and a dignity in his air and expression that procured profound respect." A fine looking man, tall, very erect, and had a prominent large nose. He lived his entire life in Farmington, dying there in 1746 at age 81.

career.notes: Served as justice and register of Farmington
From Farmington. Served as Clerk two sessions; Speaker six sessions.
Served as judge of Superior Court concurrent with assistant role
Commissioner of the Mohegan's sequestered land at New London

cross_connections.display_label: Both married Hooker brothers — from two separate Stanley families.
Their families united — three daughters married Hart brothers.
Her sister Elizabeth Standley married into the Wadsworth line.

narrative_blocks.header: Her father won distinction in the Indian wars
narrative_blocks.body: Capt. John Standley was a man of wealth and high social position who had served as lieutenant and captain in the Indian wars. Her sister Elizabeth Standley's granddaughter married Col. Jeremiah Wadsworth, Commissary General of the Continental Army — Wadsworth's wife also descended from Rev. Thomas Hooker, making Wadsworth's children doubly connected to the Hooker line.

bio.notes: Also spelled 'Stanley' in EH entry 30; 'Standley' in entries 99-101

burial.plot_notes: Memento Mori Cemetery, Farmington CT. SOURCE: FindAGrave.

children_noted: I00041 Mercy/Mary Hart — married John Hooker Jr. (H00098) July 4, 1728

sources.notes: Barbour Collection: "Mary, d. Dea. Ebenezer, m. John HOOKER, Jr., July 4, 1728"

open_questions.question: Deacon Thomas Hart vs Deacon Ebenezer Hart — identity of Mercy Hart's father
open_questions.notes: EH entry 98 gives Mercy Hart's father as 'Deacon Thomas Hart'. FVR (Barbour Collection) gives 'John, Jr., m. Mary HEART, d. Dea. Ebenezer' — suggesting Deacon Ebenezer Hart. Could be two different Deacons, or EH/FVR error on first name. Andrews' Stephen Hart and his Descendants should resolve this.
open_questions.action: Consult Andrews' Stephen Hart genealogy; check Kensington church deacon records

sources.full: Alfred Andrews, Genealogical History of Deacon Stephen Hart and his Descendants (New Britain, CT), pp. 364-5

burial.plot_notes: Christian Lane Cemetery, Kensington, Hartford County, Connecticut

parents.father_research_notes: Capt. Thomas Hart of Farmington — son of Stephen Hart (X00034), deacon of Rev. Thomas Hooker's church. Capt. Thomas Hart b. ca. 1644, d. Aug 27, 1726, married Ruth Hawkins.

political_offices.title: Representative
political_offices.notes: Represented town of Farmington often in the General Court. EH source: 'man of great dignity and influence'

church_roles.significance: First of four consecutive generations of deacons in this family: Thomas³ → Elijah⁴ → Elijah⁵ → Elijah⁶
church_roles.ordination_charge: I charge thee before God and the Lord Jesus Christ... that thou be faithful to the trust that is committed unto thee; thou art made a steward of the external good things of this Church.

narrative_blocks.header: Son of Capt. Thomas Hart
narrative_blocks.body: Son of Capt. Thomas Hart — grandson of Stephen Hart, deacon of Rev. Thomas Hooker's original church

narrative_blocks.header: Deacon of Kensington church from 1718/9
narrative_blocks.body: Deacon of Kensington church from 1718/9 — first of four generations of deacons in the Hart family

narrative_blocks.header: Meeting house of Kensington built on his home
narrative_blocks.body: Meeting house of Kensington built on his home lot by order of the Connecticut Legislature, May session 1732

narrative_blocks.header: Justice of the Peace and town representative
narrative_blocks.body: Justice of the Peace and town representative to General Court

narrative_blocks.header: Among original ten members of Kensington church
narrative_blocks.body: Among original ten members of Kensington church at its founding December 10, 1712

narrative_blocks.header: His father Capt. Thomas Hart and John Hooker
narrative_blocks.body: His father Capt. Thomas Hart and John Hooker were described as 'the two prominent men of the town'
